exercises.title: Insert Documents with insertOne and insertMany
exercises.problemHtml:
A MongoDB collection stores JSON-like documents, and everything begins with getting data in. insertOne adds a single document and insertMany adds several in one call. Each returns an acknowledgement telling you the write succeeded and the _id values assigned — either the ones you supplied or ones MongoDB generated.

Working in the products collection, do the following in order:

  • Insert one document with insertOne: { _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9, tags: ["input", "wired"] }.
  • Insert two more with insertMany: { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19 } and { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150 }.
  • Return the total number of documents with countDocuments — it should be 3.

Supply explicit integer _id values so the results are predictable; without them MongoDB assigns a random ObjectId. The scaffold provides the three calls to complete.
exercises.inputSpec: An empty products collection. You issue mongosh commands (equivalent to the Node driver collection methods).
exercises.outputSpec: insertOne returns { acknowledged: true, insertedId: 1 }; insertMany returns { acknowledged: true, insertedIds: { "0": 2, "1": 3 } }; countDocuments returns 3.
exercises.constraints: Use insertOne and insertMany (the modern methods) — not the deprecated insert(). Supply explicit integer _id values.

examplesJson.explanation: The write is acknowledged and the assigned _id (the one you supplied, 1) is echoed back.
insertMany reports each inserted _id keyed by its position in the input array.

hintsJson: Two methods cover one-or-many inserts; both return an acknowledgement with the ids.
insertOne(doc) adds a single document; insertMany([...]) adds an array of them.
countDocuments() with no filter counts everything in the collection.
db.products.insertOne({ _id: 1, ... }); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 2, ... }, { _id: 3, ... }]); db.products.countDocuments().

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.insertOne({ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9, tags: ["input", "wired"] })
db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19 }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150 }])
db.products.countDocuments()

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The two insert methods are the foundation of writing data. insertOne takes a single document object and returns { acknowledged: true, insertedId: 1 } — acknowledged confirms the server received and applied the write, and insertedId reports the document's _id. insertMany takes an array and returns the ids keyed by array position in insertedIds. Because you supplied explicit integer _ids, the results are predictable; had you omitted them, each document would receive a generated 12-byte ObjectId instead, which is the normal production choice but non-deterministic.

The key design point is that _id is mandatory and unique per collection — it is the primary key, always indexed, and inserting a second document with an existing _id raises a duplicate-key error (E11000). The common beginner mistake is reaching for the old insert() method seen in dated tutorials; it is deprecated in favour of the explicit insertOne/insertMany pair, which have clearer semantics and return values. A second subtlety: insertMany is ordered by default, so if one document in the middle fails, the ones after it are not inserted; pass { ordered: false } to attempt every document regardless. countDocuments() with an empty filter returns the total, confirming all three writes landed.

exercises.title: Query Documents with find, findOne, and Projections
exercises.problemHtml:
Reading is where MongoDB queries earn their keep. find returns every document matching a filter (as a cursor you materialise with toArray), findOne returns just the first match, and a projection selects which fields come back — trimming payloads to what you actually need.

The products collection is pre-seeded with three documents. Do the following:

  • Find all products with price greater than 20, returning only the name field and suppressing _id — projection { name: 1, _id: 0 } — as an array.
  • Fetch the single product with _id: 2 using findOne.
  • Fetch the Monitor by name, projecting only its price (no _id).
  • Return all products with just name and price, sorted by price ascending.
  • Count how many products have price greater than 20.

Remember that _id is included by default and must be explicitly turned off with _id: 0. The scaffold provides the query skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: A products collection seeded with { _id:1, name:"Keyboard", price:49.9 }, { _id:2, name:"Mouse", price:19 }, { _id:3, name:"Monitor", price:150 }.
exercises.outputSpec: The projected find returns [{ name: 'Keyboard' }, { name: 'Monitor' }]; findOne({_id:2}) returns the Mouse document; findOne Monitor returns { price: 150 }; the sorted find returns the three by ascending price; countDocuments({price:{$gt:20}}) returns 2.
exercises.constraints: Use a projection to limit fields; suppress _id with _id: 0. Use toArray() to materialise find() results. Sort with { price: 1 } for ascending.

examplesJson.explanation: Only Keyboard (49.9) and Monitor (150) exceed 20; the projection returns just their name and drops _id.
findOne returns a single document (not an array) — the one matching _id 2.

hintsJson: find returns many, findOne returns one; a second argument to find selects fields.
The projection { name: 1, _id: 0 } keeps name and explicitly removes the always-on _id.
find() gives a cursor; call .toArray() to get a plain array of documents.
db.products.find({ price: { $gt: 20 } }, { name: 1, _id: 0 }).toArray(); db.products.findOne({ _id: 2 }).

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.products.drop(); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9 }, { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19 }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150 }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.find({ price: { $gt: 20 } }, { name: 1, _id: 0 }).toArray()
db.products.findOne({ _id: 2 })
db.products.findOne({ name: "Monitor" }, { price: 1, _id: 0 })
db.products.find({}, { name: 1, price: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ price: 1 }).toArray()
db.products.countDocuments({ price: { $gt: 20 } })

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
find and findOne differ in shape: find returns a cursor over all matches, which you turn into a concrete array with toArray(), whereas findOne returns a single document object (or null if nothing matches). The first argument to either is the query filter — { price: { $gt: 20 } } uses the $gt operator to mean "greater than 20" — and the second argument to find is the projection.

The projection is where the important rule lives: fields are opt-in with 1, but _id is the exception — it is returned by default and must be explicitly suppressed with _id: 0. Forgetting that is the single most common projection surprise: you ask for { name: 1 } expecting only names and get { _id: ..., name: ... } back. Note also that you generally cannot mix inclusion and exclusion in one projection (e.g. { name: 1, price: 0 } is an error) — the only permitted mix is suppressing _id alongside inclusions, which is exactly what you do here. Projecting narrowly matters at scale because it reduces the bytes transferred over the wire and can let a query be served entirely from an index (a covered query) without touching the documents.

exercises.title: Modify a Document with updateOne and $set
exercises.problemHtml:
Updating in MongoDB is deliberately explicit: you name the document to change with a filter, and you describe the change with an update operator. The workhorse operator is $set, which assigns fields without disturbing the rest of the document. The result tells you how many documents matched and how many actually changed.

In the seeded products collection:

  • Use updateOne to set the price of the product with _id: 2 to 21.5 and add a new field onSale: true — both in a single $set — then read it back with findOne.
  • Run the same $set of price to 21.5 again and note the result reports modifiedCount: 0 — it matched but changed nothing.
  • Use updateOne to rename the product with _id: 1 to "Keyboard Pro" and set its price to 45, then read it back.

Observe the update result: matchedCount is 1 (one document matched the filter) and modifiedCount is 1 when it actually changed, or 0 when the value was already what you set. The scaffold provides the update skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: A products collection seeded with { _id:2, name:"Mouse", price:19 } among others.
exercises.outputSpec: updateOne returns a result with matchedCount:1 and modifiedCount:1; findOne({_id:2}) then shows price 21.5 and onSale:true added.
exercises.constraints: Use $set to change fields. A bare update document without an operator would replace the whole document — do not do that. Change exactly the _id: 2 document.

examplesJson.output: { acknowledged: true, insertedId: null, matchedCount: 1, modifiedCount: 1, upsertedCount: 0 }
examplesJson.explanation: One document matched (matchedCount 1) and was changed (modifiedCount 1); nothing was inserted.
$set updated price and added onSale without touching name — the rest of the document is preserved.

hintsJson: You need to change two fields on one document without wiping the others.
The update must use an operator; $set assigns fields and leaves everything else alone.
$set can create a field that did not exist yet (onSale) as well as change one that did (price).
db.products.updateOne({ _id: 2 }, { $set: { price: 21.5, onSale: true } }).

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.products.drop(); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9 }, { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19 }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150 }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.updateOne({ _id: 2 }, { $set: { price: 21.5, onSale: true } })
db.products.findOne({ _id: 2 })
db.products.updateOne({ _id: 2 }, { $set: { price: 21.5 } })
db.products.updateOne({ _id: 1 }, { $set: { name: "Keyboard Pro", price: 45 } })
db.products.findOne({ _id: 1 })

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
updateOne changes at most one document — the first that matches the filter — and the change is expressed with update operators, here $set. $set assigns the listed fields and leaves every other field untouched, which is why name survives while price is changed and onSale is newly added ($set creates a field that did not previously exist). The result document reports matchedCount: 1 and modifiedCount: 1: the first says the filter found a document, the second says it was actually altered.

The distinction between those two counts matters. If you $set a field to the value it already holds, matchedCount is 1 but modifiedCount is 0 — the document matched but nothing changed, which is useful for detecting no-op writes. The dangerous mistake this exercise guards against is omitting the operator: updateOne({ _id: 2 }, { price: 21.5 }) is interpreted as a replacement document and would wipe out name and every other field, leaving only _id and price. Always wrap changes in an operator like $set unless you truly intend a full replace (for which the explicit replaceOne exists). This "modify in place" model is what makes partial updates cheap and concurrent-safe.

exercises.title: Update Many Documents and Upsert a Missing One
exercises.problemHtml:
Two everyday needs go beyond a single-document update: changing every document that matches a condition, and "update it if it exists, otherwise create it". updateMany handles the first; the upsert: true option handles the second, turning an update that matched nothing into an insert.

In the seeded products collection:

  • Use updateMany to add { category: "electronics" } to every document (filter {}). Confirm the result modifies all three.
  • Use updateOne with { upsert: true } to set name and price on a product with _id: 99 that does not exist yet — because no document matches, it is inserted. The result reports upsertedId: 99.
  • Return countDocuments() — now 4.

Watch the result fields: updateMany shows modifiedCount: 3, and the upsert shows upsertedCount: 1 with upsertedId. The scaffold provides the skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: A products collection seeded with three documents (_id 1, 2, 3). _id 99 does not exist.
exercises.outputSpec: updateMany({}, ...) returns matchedCount:3, modifiedCount:3; the upsert returns upsertedCount:1 and upsertedId:99; countDocuments() returns 4.
exercises.constraints: Use updateMany with an empty filter to touch all documents. Use { upsert: true } to create the missing _id: 99 document. Do not insert it manually.

examplesJson.output: { acknowledged: true, insertedId: null, matchedCount: 3, modifiedCount: 3, upsertedCount: 0 }
examplesJson.explanation: The empty filter {} matches every document, so all three gain the category field.

examplesJson.output: { acknowledged: true, insertedId: null, matchedCount: 0, modifiedCount: 0, upsertedCount: 1, upsertedId: 99 }
examplesJson.explanation: No document matched _id 99, so upsert inserts one; upsertedCount is 1 and upsertedId reports the new _id.

hintsJson: One operation changes all matches; a flag turns a no-match update into an insert.
updateMany({}, { $set: {...} }) applies the change to every document because {} matches all.
Passing { upsert: true } as the third argument inserts a new document when the filter matches nothing.
The upsert result carries upsertedId (the new _id) and upsertedCount: 1 instead of a modifiedCount.

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.products.drop(); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9 }, { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19 }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150 }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.updateMany({}, { $set: { category: "electronics" } })
db.products.updateOne({ _id: 99 }, { $set: { name: "Webcam", price: 60 } }, { upsert: true })
db.products.findOne({ _id: 99 })
db.products.countDocuments()

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
updateMany applies the same operator update to every document its filter matches; with the empty filter {} that means all three documents receive category: "electronics", so matchedCount and modifiedCount are both 3. This is the bulk-edit primitive — change a status across a whole set, backfill a new field — and it is a single round trip regardless of how many documents it touches.

The upsert: true option changes the semantics of a non-matching update. Normally updateOne({ _id: 99 }, ...) against a missing document matches nothing and does nothing (matchedCount: 0). With upsert enabled, that same miss becomes an insert: MongoDB creates a new document combining the filter's equality conditions (_id: 99) with the $set values, and the result reports upsertedCount: 1 and the new upsertedId. This is the idiomatic way to write "ensure a document exists with these values" in one atomic operation, avoiding the classic race between a separate existence check and insert. The subtle rule to remember: the filter's equality fields are carried into the inserted document, so an upsert filtered on { _id: 99 } gives the new document that id, whereas filtering on a range like { price: { $gt: 5 } } would not contribute price to the insert — you must $set everything the new document needs.

exercises.title: Remove Documents with deleteOne and deleteMany
exercises.problemHtml:
Deleting mirrors updating: you name what to remove with a filter, and MongoDB reports how many documents went. deleteOne removes at most the first match; deleteMany removes all matches. A filter that matches nothing simply deletes nothing — no error — which makes deletes safe to retry.

The products collection is seeded with five documents of varying price. Do the following:

  • Use deleteOne to remove the single product with _id: 3; the result reports deletedCount: 1.
  • Use deleteMany to remove every product with price less than 25; the result reports how many were removed.
  • Call deleteOne({ _id: 999 }) for a document that does not exist and observe deletedCount: 0.
  • Return countDocuments() to confirm what remains.

The seed has prices 49.9, 19, 150, 12, 30. After deleting _id: 3 and everything under 25, work out which documents survive. The scaffold provides the skeletons.
exercises.outputSpec: deleteOne({_id:3}) returns deletedCount:1; deleteMany({price:{$lt:25}}) returns deletedCount:2 (ids 2 and 4); deleteOne({_id:999}) returns deletedCount:0; countDocuments() returns 2 (ids 1 and 5).
exercises.constraints: Use deleteOne for the single removal and deleteMany for the price filter. Do not drop the whole collection. Expect a no-match delete to return deletedCount: 0.

examplesJson.explanation: Prices under 25 are the Mouse (19) and _id 4 (12) — two documents removed.
No document has _id 999, so nothing is deleted and deletedCount is 0 — deletes are safe to repeat.

hintsJson: deleteOne removes a single match; deleteMany removes all matches of the filter.
The filter { price: { $lt: 25 } } selects documents cheaper than 25.
A delete whose filter matches nothing returns deletedCount: 0 rather than erroring.
After removing _id 3 and everything under 25 (ids 2 and 4), the survivors are ids 1 (49.9) and 5 (30).

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.products.drop(); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9 }, { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19 }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150 }, { _id: 4, name: "Cable", price: 12 }, { _id: 5, name: "Pad", price: 30 }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.deleteOne({ _id: 3 })
db.products.deleteMany({ price: { $lt: 25 } })
db.products.deleteOne({ _id: 999 })
db.products.deleteMany({ price: { $gt: 1000 } })
db.products.countDocuments()
db.products.find({}, { _id: 1 }).sort({ _id: 1 }).toArray()

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The delete methods parallel the update methods exactly. deleteOne removes the first document matching its filter and reports deletedCount: 1 when it found one; deleteMany removes every match, so { price: { $lt: 25 } } takes out both the 19 and the 12 documents for a deletedCount: 2. Tracing the data: starting from five documents, removing _id: 3 and then everything under 25 (ids 2 and 4) leaves ids 1 (49.9) and 5 (30), so the final countDocuments() is 2.

The behaviour worth internalising is that a filter matching nothing is not an error — deleteOne({ _id: 999 }) returns deletedCount: 0 and moves on. That makes deletes idempotent and safe to retry after a network hiccup, the same property you rely on with Redis DEL or SQL's forgiving deletes. The genuinely dangerous mistake is the empty filter: deleteMany({}) deletes every document in the collection, and unlike drop() it does so document by document while leaving indexes intact. Because the filter is what scopes the damage, always double-check it before running a delete in production — an accidental {} or a filter that is broader than intended is the classic data-loss incident. For removing an entire collection and its indexes, the explicit drop() is clearer and faster than deleteMany({}).

exercises.title: Filter with Query Operators: $gt, $lte, $in, and $ne
exercises.problemHtml:
Real queries rarely test plain equality. MongoDB's query operators — all prefixed with $ — express ranges, membership, and negation inside the filter document. Combining several fields in one filter implicitly ANDs them, giving you precise selection without any query language beyond JSON.

The products collection is seeded with a price and a category per document. Write three queries, each returning an array of names only (projection { name: 1, _id: 0 }):

  • Products with price greater than 20 and at most 100 — combine $gt and $lte on the same field.
  • Products whose category is in the set ["input", "display"] — use $in.
  • Products whose category is not "cable" — use $ne.

Sort each result by name ascending so the output order is stable. The scaffold provides the three query skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: A products collection seeded with { name, price, category } documents spanning several prices and categories (input, display, cable).
exercises.outputSpec: Three name-only arrays: the price range 20..100, the category $in [input, display], and category $ne cable, each sorted by name.
exercises.constraints: Use $gt/$lte for the range on one field, $in for membership, $ne for negation. Project only name (no _id) and sort by name ascending.

examplesJson.explanation: Two conditions on price combine as a range: Keyboard (49.9) and Pad (30) are both above 20 and at most 100; Monitor (150) is excluded.
$in matches any document whose category is one of the listed values.

hintsJson: A range on one field is two operators in the same sub-document; membership and negation are their own operators.
Combine bounds like { price: { $gt: 20, $lte: 100 } } — both apply to price.
$in takes an array of allowed values; $ne takes a single value to exclude.
Add .sort({ name: 1 }) before .toArray() so results come back in a stable order.

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.products.drop(); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9, category: "input" }, { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19, category: "input" }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150, category: "display" }, { _id: 4, name: "Cable", price: 12, category: "cable" }, { _id: 5, name: "Pad", price: 30, category: "desk" }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.find({ price: { $gt: 20, $lte: 100 } }, { name: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ name: 1 }).toArray()
db.products.find({ category: { $in: ["input", "display"] } }, { name: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ name: 1 }).toArray()
db.products.find({ category: { $ne: "cable" } }, { name: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ name: 1 }).toArray()

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Query operators turn the filter document into a small expression language. Two operators on the same field, { price: { $gt: 20, $lte: 100 } }, are ANDed into a range — greater than 20 and at most 100 — so Keyboard (49.9) and Pad (30) qualify while Monitor (150) does not. $in tests membership against an array of allowed values, and $ne negates a single value. When a filter lists several fields, those are implicitly ANDed too, which is why you rarely need an explicit $and.

Two traps are worth flagging. First, operator boundaries: $gt/$lt are exclusive while $gte/$lte are inclusive, so $lte: 100 would include a product priced exactly 100 whereas $lt: 100 would not — off-by-one filtering bugs almost always trace back to this choice. Second, $ne and $nin have a subtle reach: they also match documents where the field is absent, because a missing category is "not equal to cable" by MongoDB's logic, which can pull in documents you did not expect; guard with $exists if that matters. Finally, these operators are what indexes accelerate — an index on price lets the range query skip a full collection scan — and the .sort({ name: 1 }) here only orders the output, distinct from the filter that selects it.

exercises.title: Change Numbers and Arrays with $inc, $push, and $addToSet
exercises.problemHtml:
Beyond $set, MongoDB has operators that modify values in place: $inc adjusts a number atomically, and the array operators grow or shrink an embedded array without you reading it first. This lets you increment a counter, append to a log, or maintain a tag set entirely server-side, safely under concurrency.

The posts collection has one document { _id: 1, title: "Intro", likes: 0, tags: ["draft"] }. Apply these updates to _id: 1:

  • $inc the likes field by 3.
  • $push the value "mongodb" onto tags (append, allowing duplicates).
  • $addToSet the value "draft" onto tags — because it already exists, the array is unchanged (set semantics).
  • $pull the value "draft" from tags (remove all occurrences).
  • Read the document back with findOne.

Predict the final tags array and likes before you run it. The scaffold provides the update skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: A posts collection with one document { _id: 1, title: "Intro", likes: 0, tags: ["draft"] }.
exercises.outputSpec: After the updates, findOne({_id:1}) shows likes: 3 and tags: ["mongodb"] (draft was added-as-duplicate-noop by $addToSet then removed by $pull; mongodb remains from $push).
exercises.constraints: Use $inc for the number and $push / $addToSet / $pull for the array. Do not read-modify-write the array in application code.

examplesJson.output: { acknowledged: true, insertedId: null, matchedCount: 1, modifiedCount: 1, upsertedCount: 0 }
examplesJson.explanation: $inc adds 3 to likes atomically, taking it from 0 to 3.
$push added "mongodb"; $addToSet("draft") was a no-op since draft was present; $pull("draft") removed the original draft, leaving only mongodb.

hintsJson: Each change has a dedicated operator so the server does the read-modify-write atomically.
$inc: { likes: 3 } adds to a number; a negative value subtracts.
$push always appends (duplicates allowed); $addToSet appends only if the value is absent.
$pull: { tags: "draft" } removes every occurrence of "draft" from the array.

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.posts.drop(); db.posts.insertOne({ _id: 1, title: "Intro", likes: 0, tags: ["draft"] })

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.posts.updateOne({ _id: 1 }, { $inc: { likes: 3 } })
db.posts.updateOne({ _id: 1 }, { $push: { tags: "mongodb" } })
db.posts.updateOne({ _id: 1 }, { $addToSet: { tags: "draft" } })
db.posts.updateOne({ _id: 1 }, { $pull: { tags: "draft" } })
db.posts.findOne({ _id: 1 })

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
These operators modify a document in place on the server, which is both concise and concurrency-safe. $inc adds to a numeric field atomically — likes goes 0 → 3 — so two simultaneous increments never lose a count, exactly the guarantee an application-side read-modify-write cannot make. The array operators similarly avoid fetching the array first: $push appends unconditionally (duplicates allowed), $addToSet appends only if the value is not already present, and $pull removes every element equal to the given value.

Tracing the tags array is the point of the exercise. It starts as ["draft"]. $push "mongodb" makes it ["draft", "mongodb"]. $addToSet "draft" is a no-op because "draft" is already there — that is the difference from $push, which would have produced a duplicate. Then $pull "draft" removes the original, leaving ["mongodb"]. The common confusion is expecting $addToSet and $push to behave the same; use $addToSet for tag-like sets where duplicates are meaningless, and $push for logs or event lists where every entry counts (and where $push with the $each, $slice, and $sort modifiers can even maintain a capped, sorted array). Because each operator is atomic, this is how you maintain counters and collections under load without transactions.

exercises.title: Sort, Limit, Skip, and Summarise with distinct
exercises.problemHtml:
Presenting data means ordering and paging it. Cursor modifiers sort, limit, and skip chain onto a find to produce "top N" lists and pages, while distinct collapses a field to its unique values. Together they cover the read-side needs of most list views.

The products collection is seeded with five documents. Produce these results:

  • The two most expensive products, names and prices only, using sort({ price: -1 }).limit(2).
  • The "second page" of one product when ordered by price descending: sort({ price: -1 }).skip(2).limit(1).
  • The list of distinct category values with distinct("category").
  • The total document count with countDocuments().

Sort direction is 1 for ascending and -1 for descending. The scaffold provides the query skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: A products collection seeded with five { name, price, category } documents across categories input, display, cable, desk.
exercises.outputSpec: Top-2 by price returns Monitor then Keyboard; skip(2).limit(1) returns the 3rd most expensive (Pad); distinct("category") returns the unique categories; countDocuments() returns 5.
exercises.constraints: Use sort with -1 for descending. Chain skip before limit for paging. Use distinct for unique values, not a manual loop.

examplesJson.explanation: Descending by price puts Monitor (150) first and Keyboard (49.9) second; limit(2) keeps just those two.
distinct collapses the category field to its unique values (returned sorted).

hintsJson: Ordering, paging, and unique-values are three separate tools that chain onto or replace find.
sort({ price: -1 }) orders high-to-low; limit(n) keeps the first n after sorting.
For a page, skip past the earlier pages then limit: .skip(2).limit(1) is the third item.
distinct("category") returns an array of the unique values of that field.

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.products.drop(); db.products.insertMany([{ _id: 1, name: "Keyboard", price: 49.9, category: "input" }, { _id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 19, category: "input" }, { _id: 3, name: "Monitor", price: 150, category: "display" }, { _id: 4, name: "Cable", price: 12, category: "cable" }, { _id: 5, name: "Pad", price: 30, category: "desk" }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.products.find({}, { name: 1, price: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ price: -1 }).limit(2).toArray()
db.products.find({}, { name: 1, price: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ price: -1 }).skip(2).limit(1).toArray()
db.products.distinct("category")
db.products.countDocuments()

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
These cursor modifiers compose to produce ordered pages. sort({ price: -1 }) orders results high-to-low (-1 descending, 1 ascending); limit(2) then keeps only the first two, giving the two most expensive products. For pagination you combine skip and limit: skip(2).limit(1) discards the top two and returns the next one — the third most expensive. distinct("category") is a different shape entirely: rather than returning documents, it returns the unique values a field takes, which MongoDB returns sorted.

The critical detail is evaluation order. Regardless of how you chain them, the server applies sort, then skip, then limit — so the sort establishes a stable ordering before paging carves out a slice. If you omit the sort, skip/limit operate on an undefined order and "page 2" can overlap or miss rows between requests; always sort on a unique or tie-broken key when paginating. The well-known scaling caveat is that skip still walks past the skipped documents internally, so deep pagination (skip(100000)) grows linearly slower; production systems switch to range-based pagination (find({ price: { $lt: lastSeenPrice } }).limit(n)) for large offsets. For counting, countDocuments() returns an accurate total by scanning the matched set, unlike the older count() which could return stale metadata estimates.

exercises.title: Atomically Read and Modify with findOneAndUpdate
exercises.problemHtml:
Sometimes you must change a document and see the resulting value in one indivisible step — allocating the next sequence number, claiming a job, decrementing stock. Doing a find then a separate updateOne opens a race window. findOneAndUpdate closes it: it matches, updates, and returns the document atomically, and you choose whether you get the version before or after the change.

Build an atomic counter in a counters collection:

  • Call findOneAndUpdate on { _id: "orderId" } with { $inc: { seq: 1 } }, options { upsert: true, returnDocument: "after" }. The first call creates the counter and returns the document with seq: 1.
  • Call it again with the same arguments — it now returns seq: 2.
  • Call it a third time — returns seq: 3.

Each call hands back the post-increment value, which is exactly what a "give me the next id" service needs. The scaffold provides the call to complete.
exercises.inputSpec: An empty counters collection. The document { _id: "orderId" } does not exist on the first call.
exercises.outputSpec: Three findOneAndUpdate calls return the counter document with seq 1, then 2, then 3 — the value after each increment. The first call creates it via upsert.
exercises.constraints: Use findOneAndUpdate with returnDocument: "after" and upsert: true. Do not use a separate find + update (that is not atomic). $inc must be the operator.

examplesJson.explanation: The counter did not exist, so upsert creates it and $inc sets seq to 1; returnDocument "after" gives the post-increment value.

examplesJson.input: The same call, run a second time
examplesJson.explanation: The existing counter is incremented atomically to 2 and the updated document is returned.

hintsJson: A find then update has a gap where another client can act. You need the read and write fused.
findOneAndUpdate applies the update and returns the document in one atomic operation.
Pass returnDocument: "after" to get the post-update value (the default is the pre-update "before").
Add upsert: true so the very first call creates the counter document instead of matching nothing.

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.counters.findOneAndUpdate({ _id: "orderId" }, { $inc: { seq: 1 } }, { upsert: true, returnDocument: "after" })
db.counters.findOneAndUpdate({ _id: "orderId" }, { $inc: { seq: 1 } }, { upsert: true, returnDocument: "after" })
db.counters.findOneAndUpdate({ _id: "orderId" }, { $inc: { seq: 1 } }, { upsert: true, returnDocument: "after" })

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
findOneAndUpdate fuses three steps — locate, modify, return — into one atomic server operation, which is what makes it the correct tool for a sequence generator. The { $inc: { seq: 1 } } bumps the counter, returnDocument: "after" asks for the value after the increment, and upsert: true means the first call, which matches no existing document, creates { _id: "orderId", seq: 1 } rather than returning null. Each subsequent call increments and hands back seq: 2, then seq: 3. No two concurrent callers can ever receive the same number, because the increment-and-read happens under the document's lock with no gap.

Contrast the naive approach: findOne to read seq, add one in your code, then updateOne to write it back. Between the read and the write, another client can read the same value, and both write the same "next" id — a duplicate that corrupts whatever the id keys. findOneAndUpdate eliminates that window entirely. Two options are easy to get wrong: returnDocument defaults to "before" (the pre-update document, or null on an upsert's first run), so you must pass "after" to see the incremented value; and in older drivers the option was the boolean returnNewDocument or returnOriginal, which is why copied snippets sometimes return the wrong version. The same atomic pattern — match on a condition, mutate, return — underlies job queues (claim a pending task) and inventory (decrement only if stock remains, by adding the stock check to the filter).
exercises.diagramMermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App
  participant Mongo
  App->>Mongo: findOneAndUpdate inc seq return after
  Mongo-->>App: document with seq 1
  App->>Mongo: findOneAndUpdate inc seq return after
  Mongo-->>App: document with seq 2

exercises.title: Apply a Batch of Mixed Writes with bulkWrite
exercises.problemHtml:
When a request needs several different writes — insert two rows, adjust one, remove another — sending them one at a time costs a round trip each. bulkWrite submits a mixed batch of operations in a single call and returns a combined tally of what each kind of operation did. It is the throughput tool for write-heavy paths.

The inventory collection is seeded with { _id: 1, sku: "A", qty: 5 } and { _id: 2, sku: "B", qty: 0 }. Issue one bulkWrite containing, in order:

  • insertOne a new item { _id: 3, sku: "C", qty: 8 }.
  • updateOne matching { _id: 1 } with { $inc: { qty: -2 } } (ship two of item A).
  • deleteOne matching { _id: 2 } (drop the out-of-stock item B).
  • An updateOne with upsert: true matching { _id: 4 }, setting { sku: "D", qty: 3 }.

Then read the whole collection back with find().sort({ _id: 1 }).toArray(). Predict the batch result counts before running it. The scaffold provides the bulkWrite skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: An inventory collection seeded with { _id:1, sku:"A", qty:5 } and { _id:2, sku:"B", qty:0 }.
exercises.outputSpec: bulkWrite returns insertedCount:1, modifiedCount:1, deletedCount:1, upsertedCount:1. The final collection is _id 1 (qty 3), _id 3 (qty 8), _id 4 (qty 3) — _id 2 deleted.
exercises.constraints: Use a single bulkWrite with insertOne, updateOne ($inc), deleteOne, and an upserting updateOne. Do not issue four separate calls. Keep the operations in the listed order.

examplesJson.input: db.inventory.bulkWrite([ { insertOne: {...} }, { updateOne: { filter: {_id:1}, update: { $inc: { qty: -2 } } } }, { deleteOne: { filter: {_id:2} } }, { updateOne: { filter: {_id:4}, update: { $set: {...} }, upsert: true } } ])
examplesJson.output: result with insertedCount: 1, matchedCount: 1, modifiedCount: 1, deletedCount: 1, upsertedCount: 1
examplesJson.explanation: Each operation contributes to its own counter: one insert, one modify, one delete, one upsert.

examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 1, sku: 'A', qty: 3 }, { _id: 3, sku: 'C', qty: 8 }, { _id: 4, qty: 3, sku: 'D' } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Item A dropped from 5 to 3, C was inserted, D was upserted, and B (_id 2) was deleted. (In the upserted D document MongoDB orders the $set fields after the _id, so it prints as qty then sku.)

hintsJson: Four different writes in one request means one array of operation descriptors, not four calls.
Each element names its operation: { insertOne: { document } }, { updateOne: { filter, update } }, { deleteOne: { filter } }.
The upsert goes inside the updateOne descriptor: { updateOne: { filter, update, upsert: true } }.
The result carries separate insertedCount, modifiedCount, deletedCount, and upsertedCount fields.

starterCodeJson.name: seed.js
starterCodeJson.code:
// Run once in mongosh to create the data this exercise works with.
db.inventory.drop(); db.inventory.insertMany([{ _id: 1, sku: "A", qty: 5 }, { _id: 2, sku: "B", qty: 0 }])

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js
solutionCodeJson.code:
db.inventory.bulkWrite([
  { insertOne: { document: { _id: 3, sku: "C", qty: 8 } } },
  { updateOne: { filter: { _id: 1 }, update: { $inc: { qty: -2 } } } },
  { deleteOne: { filter: { _id: 2 } } },
  { updateOne: { filter: { _id: 4 }, update: { $set: { sku: "D", qty: 3 } }, upsert: true } }
])
db.inventory.find().sort({ _id: 1 }).toArray()

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
bulkWrite takes an array of operation descriptors, each an object whose single key names the operation — insertOne, updateOne, deleteOne, replaceOne — and whose value carries that operation's arguments. Submitting them together means one network round trip instead of four, and the returned result aggregates the outcome into separate counters: insertedCount: 1 from the insert, modifiedCount: 1 from the $inc, deletedCount: 1 from the delete, and upsertedCount: 1 from the upserting update. Tracing the data confirms it: item A goes 5 → 3, B (_id 2) is removed, C is inserted, and D is upserted, leaving three documents ordered 1, 3, 4.

The behaviour to understand is ordering and atomicity. By default bulkWrite is ordered: operations execute in sequence and, if one fails, the remaining operations are abandoned — useful when later writes depend on earlier ones. Passing { ordered: false } lets MongoDB run them in any order and continue past failures, which is faster and appropriate for independent writes. The crucial caveat is that bulkWrite is not a transaction: each individual operation is atomic, but the batch as a whole is not, so a mid-batch failure in ordered mode can leave earlier operations applied and later ones not. When you need all-or-nothing across multiple documents, wrap the writes in a real multi-document transaction (a session with startTransaction) instead. For the common case of many independent writes on a hot path, bulkWrite is the right, and much faster, choice.
